claim.hypotheses_location: Abstract; Section 1 Introduction (e.g., statements: 'Accordingly, it was hypothesized that people would increase their intimacy with a partner who was attentive to them.' and 'we tested the hypothesis that people adjust their intimacy with a partner in accordance with how much attention the partner pays to them.')

data.source: Scenario-based questionnaires (Study 1a, 1b) and laboratory experiments with a computerized joint task (Study 2a–2c)
data.sample_size: Study 1a: 312 Japanese undergraduates (3 omitted; final N≈309). Study 1b: 105 undergraduates. Study 2a: 29 undergraduates (after excluding 1). Study 2b: 44 undergraduates (after excluding 1). Study 2c: 36 undergraduates (after excluding 2).
data.notes: Study 1 used three scenario types (advice, surprise, moral support) with manipulations of attention (present/absent) and benefit (present/absent); Study 1a also manipulated time (long/short). Intimacy measured via 3 items (Cronbach’s alpha .69–.80 per scenario; aggregated alpha .76). Study 2 used a computer-mediated trivia quiz with a partner attention signal (red/blue) and disabled hints (no benefit) to dissociate attention from benefit; intimacy measured via 4 items (overall alpha .72). Attention frequency manipulated at 0%, 20%, 50%, 80% across conditions. Social exchange outcomes in Study 1: expectation of partner help, willingness to help. Interest in friendship assessed in Study 2.

method.description: The study comprises two scenario experiments (Study 1a–1b) and three laboratory experiments (Study 2a–2c) testing whether a partner’s attention increases intimacy independent of tangible benefits. In Study 1, participants read scenarios where attention and benefit were orthogonally manipulated (and time in 1a). They then rated intimacy and social exchange intentions. In Study 2, attention was implemented as a monitoring signal in a joint trivia task; hints were disabled to remove instrumental benefits. Attention frequency was manipulated across conditions, and participants rated intimacy and interest in friendship.
steps: Study 1a: Recruit undergraduate participants; present three counterbalanced scenarios (advice, surprise, moral support); manipulate attention (present/absent), benefit (present/absent), and time (long/short); collect intimacy (3 items) and social exchange measures (expectation of help, willingness to help) on 5-point scales.
Analyze Study 1a with ANOVAs collapsing scenarios and per scenario; report main effects of attention, benefit, and time (when applicable).
Study 1b: Replicate without the time factor; same measures and analyses.

Study 2 common setup: Pair participant (Player 2) with ostensible partner (computer as Player 1); explain that Player 1 can monitor Player 2 (attention signal turns blue) but hints are disabled (no benefit); complete 20 multiple-choice trivia questions; manipulate attention frequency per condition.
Study 2a: Compare attention (80% monitoring) vs no attention (0%); collect intimacy (4 items, 7-point scale) and interest in friendship.
Study 2b: Compare attention (80%, intentional) vs no intention control (80% monitoring determined by computer so partner cannot intentionally attend); collect outcomes.
Study 2c: Manipulate attention frequency at 20% (low), 50% (middle), 80% (high); assess outcomes; have participants estimate number of attention signals for manipulation check.
Conduct t-tests, ANOVAs (including Tukey’s HSD in 2c), correlations, and mediation analyses (Sobel tests) where applicable.
method.models: ANOVA (factorial and omnibus), independent-samples t-tests, Tukey’s HSD post hoc tests, Pearson correlations, mediation analyses with Sobel tests
method.outcome_variable: Primary: Intimacy score; Secondary: Expectation of partner help, Willingness to help, Interest in friendship
method.independent_variables: Attention (present vs absent; frequency 0%, 20%, 50%, 80%; intentional vs no intention), Benefit (present vs absent), Time spent (long vs short; Study 1a only), Scenario type (advice, surprise, moral support), Scenario order; Sex (tested, generally non-significant)
method.control_variables: Scenario order; No-intention control (to neutralize perceptions in Study 2b); Sex (examined but not retained due to non-significance)

results.summary: Across scenario and laboratory experiments, partner attention robustly increased reported intimacy, even when dissociated from instrumental benefits. In Study 1, attention had a larger and more consistent effect on intimacy than benefits; intimacy fully mediated effects of attention on expectation of partner help and willingness to help. In Study 2, intentional attention (80% monitoring) increased intimacy relative to no attention (0%) and to a neutral no-intention control; interest in friendship tended to increase with attention and correlated with intimacy. Intimacy rose with higher frequencies of attention (20% < 50% < 80%), and participants accurately tracked attention frequency. Time allocation (Study 1a) showed limited effects. Overall, results support that interpersonal attention fosters intimacy and downstream social exchange intentions independent of immediate benefits.

metadata.original_paper_title: It's the attention that counts: interpersonal attention fosters intimacy and social exchange
